title: VSCodeVim: How a keystroke becomes a Vim command
description:
[VSCodeVim](https://github.com/VSCodeVim/Vim) is a Vim emulation layer for Visual Studio Code, written in TypeScript. This tour follows a single thread through the codebase: what happens between you pressing a key and VSCodeVim performing a Vim action.

We start at the extension's entry point, see how it hijacks VS Code's keyboard input, then walk through the mode system, the key-to-action dispatcher, and the registry that matches key sequences to actions. We finish with two concrete examples of those actions — a motion (`h`) and an operator (`d`) — and a look at how user-defined key remappings get a first crack at every keystroke.

stops.title: The entry point: activate()
stops.content:
Every VS Code extension exposes an `activate` function that the editor calls on startup. VSCodeVim's lives in `extensionBase.ts` (shared between the desktop and web builds). Before it can interpret a single keypress it has to bootstrap the world: load the user's configuration, initialize the `Logger`, restore persisted state like registers and command history, and register itself for disposal.

The rest of `activate` (below this snippet) wires up event listeners and command overrides — the hooks that let a normally-modeless editor behave like Vim.

stops.title: Capturing every keystroke
stops.content:
This is the linchpin of the whole extension. VS Code routes printable keyboard input through a built-in command called `type`. By calling `overrideCommand` to replace it, VSCodeVim intercepts every character you type before VS Code inserts it.

Each keystroke is pushed onto a `taskQueue` (so keys are processed strictly in order, even though handlers are async), the active editor's `ModeHandler` is fetched, and — unless we're mid-IME-composition — the key is forwarded to `mh.handleKeyEvent(args.text)`. From here on, nothing is "typed" directly; it all flows through Vim's brain.

stops.title: The modes
stops.content:
Vim is modal, and this enum in `mode/mode.ts` enumerates every mode VSCodeVim understands — from `Normal`, `Insert`, and the three `Visual` flavors, to special states like `SearchInProgressMode` and plugin modes such as `EasyMotionMode`.

The current mode is the single most important piece of context for interpreting a key: pressing `d` means "delete operator" in `Normal` mode but inserts the literal letter in `Insert` mode. Note `OperatorPendingMode` is a pseudo-mode used only while resolving remaps — the comment warns you never to actually set it.

stops.title: Dispatching a key to an action
stops.content:
Inside `ModeHandler`, `handleKeyAsAnAction` is where a key sequence is resolved into something to run. The incoming key is appended to `recordedState.actionKeys` — the buffer of keys pressed so far for the current command — and that whole sequence is handed to `getRelevantAction`.

The result is a three-way decision encoded by `KeypressState`: a concrete action matched and we run it; `WaitingOnKeys` means the sequence is a valid prefix (e.g. you pressed `g` and might be building `gg`), so we keep buffering; or `NoPossibleMatch`, in which case we throw the buffer away and start fresh.

stops.title: The action registry and key matching
stops.content:
Actions register themselves at import time with the `@RegisterAction` decorator, which drops each action class into `actionMap`, keyed by every `Mode` it supports.

`getRelevantAction` then does the matching: it walks the actions registered for the current mode, constructs each one, and asks `doesActionApply` (an exact match) or `couldActionApply` (a prefix match). An exact match wins immediately; otherwise, if any action *could* still match with more keys, it returns `WaitingOnKeys`; if none could, `NoPossibleMatch`. The `TODO` cheerfully admits that rebuilding hundreds of action objects on every keystroke is wasteful.

stops.title: A motion: moving left with 'h'
stops.content:
Here's a concrete action — a `BaseMovement`. Its `keys` field lists the sequences that trigger it: `h`, the left-arrow, and a few backspace variants. The matching machinery from the previous stop compares your keypresses against exactly this declaration.

`execAction` computes the new cursor `Position`. Most of the code handles edge cases that make "move one character left" surprisingly subtle: Unicode surrogate pairs (so emoji count as one character) and whether the cursor is allowed to wrap past the start of a line, which depends on the mode and configuration via `shouldWrapKey`.

stops.title: An operator: the delete operator 'd'
stops.content:
Motions move the cursor; operators act on a range of text. `DeleteOperator` is bound to `d` and, per its `modes`, is valid in `Normal` and both visual modes. In normal mode it pairs with a motion — `dw`, `d$` — to define the `start`/`end` range its `run` method receives.

Notice the Vim-faithful details: line-wise deletes (`dd`) snap the range to whole lines, and there's careful handling of the trailing newline character, which Vim conceptually places one past the end of a line. The deleted text is also yanked into a register before removal.

stops.title: User remappings get first dibs
stops.content:
Before a key is ever matched against the built-in actions, the `Remapper` gets a chance to rewrite it. Each `Remapper` instance owns one config key (e.g. `insertModeKeyBindings`) and the modes it applies to, and `sendKey` is called on every keystroke.

It looks up the user's remappings for the current mode and asks two questions via `findMatchingRemap` and `hasPotentialRemap`: does this key sequence *exactly* match a remap, and could it *still* match if more keys arrive? That second question is why mappings like `jj → <Esc>` work — the first `j` is buffered as a "potential remap" rather than being inserted immediately. The surrounding logic in this file is largely devoted to untangling ambiguous and partial remaps and their timeouts.
